title: Ultrafast internal dynamics of chiral domain walls probed by time-resolved XRMS
authors: Dmitriy Ksenzov, Flavio Capotondi, Nicolas Jaouen, Raphael Gruber, Vincent Cros, Mathias Kläui, Matteo Pancaldi, Emanuele Pedersoli, Nicolas Reyren, Christian Gutt
abstract: We measure picosecond dynamics of labyrinthine stripe domains with chiral Néel-type domain walls using time-resolved x-ray resonant magnetic scattering (XRMS). At the stripe-domain wavevector, the helicity-summed signal shows ultrafast demagnetization, recovery, and a weak oscillatory contribution that we identify as a signature of laser-launched coherent surface phonons. In contrast, the dichroic signal, which is sensitive to the in-plane magnetization inside the Néel walls, shows a strong oscillatory response whose frequency decreases with increasing pump fluence. We attribute this softening to pump-induced changes in the effective anisotropy and saturation magnetization, which modify the restoring field of an internal domain-wall mode. Time-resolved XRMS thus isolates wall-specific dynamics and provides access to internal domain-wall motion in disordered stripe textures on picosecond time scales.
summary: This work uses time-resolved X-ray Magnetic Scattering (XRMS) to investigate how chiral domain walls evolve on picosecond timescales. By analyzing the dichroic signal, the researchers successfully isolated the dynamics of the internal domain-wall mode. The key finding is that this mode's oscillation frequency softens as the pump fluence increases, providing insight into how external energy modifies the magnetic anisotropy and restoring forces within the domain walls.
main_problem: The study aims to probe the ultrafast internal dynamics of chiral domain walls within magnetic multilayers on picosecond timescales.
main_result: The dichroic signal exhibits a strong oscillatory response whose frequency decreases with increasing pump fluence, indicating pump-induced changes in the effective anisotropy and saturation magnetization that modify the internal domain-wall mode restoring field.
method: Time-resolved x-ray resonant magnetic scattering (XRMS) is employed using femtosecond optical pumping and circularly polarized FEL probes.
model_or_system: The physical system involves magnetic multilayers with perpendicular magnetic anisotropy hosting chiral Néel-type domain walls stabilized by the interfacial Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interaction. The in-plane wall magnetization is modeled using a 1D Walker domain-wall profile.
key_observables: Helicity-summed signal ($\Delta I_+$) and the dichroic signal ($I_-$), which is sensitive to the in-plane magnetization inside the Néel walls.
important_parameters: Picosecond time scales, pump fluence, and the characteristic frequency of the internal domain-wall mode.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the dichroic term arises from charge-magnetic interference, and the frequency dependence on fluence is treated phenomenologically.
figures_summary: Figures show time-momentum maps and azimuthally integrated transient dichroic responses demonstrating the fluence dependence of the oscillation frequency for different samples.
paper_structure: The paper details the experimental setup and diagnostics, analyzes the time-resolved signals by separating contributions, and presents the quantitative findings regarding the frequency softening of the internal domain-wall mode.
why_interesting: The use of time-resolved X-ray techniques to isolate and track internal, collective excitations (like domain-wall modes) in magnetic materials provides a powerful, non-equilibrium probe of magnetic order parameters, relevant to understanding ultrafast magnetization dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG 1: (a) Schematic of the time-resolved XRMS experiment. Infrared (IR) pump pulses (𝜆𝐿= 790 𝑛𝑚) excite the multilayer. Circularly polarized FEL pulses tuned to the Fe M2,3 edge (𝜆= 22.75 𝑛𝑚) probe the response in reflection geometry at 𝜃≈45° . The sample-to-detector distance was 150 mm for sample I, 100 mm for sample II, and 75 mm for sample III. (b-d) Normalized differential sum signal, (𝐼+

FIG. 2: (a,b) Time–momentum maps of Δ𝐼+ 𝐼+𝑠𝑡𝑎𝑡𝑖𝑐 ⁄ at F = 3.0 mJ cm⁻² for samples III (a) and I (b), respectively. (c,d) Corresponding momentum–integrated responses averaged around 𝑄𝑀, shown as a function of pump–probe delay for F = 1.5 (black), 3.0 (blue), and 4.5 mJ cm⁻² (red).

FIG. 3: (a, c) Time–azimuth maps of the normalized dichroic response 𝐼−(𝑡, 𝜓)/𝐼−static, for samples I (a) and II (c) at a pump fluence of F = 3.0 mJ cm⁻². (b,d) Corresponding azimuthally integrated transient dichroic responses shown as a function of pump–probe delay for pump fluences of 1.5 (black), 3.0 (blue), and 4.5 mJ cm⁻² (red).
FIG.4: Fluence dependence of the extracted frequency for samples I (red) and II (blue). Markers show data and dashed lines show linear fits 𝑓(𝐹) = 𝑓0 −𝑘𝐹.
